content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5901           322-6441              322-6444

November 9, 1981


Victor J. Kaleta
Deputy City Attorney
100 North Garfield Avenue
Pasadena, CA 91109

Dear Mr. Kaleta:

     I am writing in response to your question concerning a
member of Pasadena's Interim Design Review Commission. The
specific facts are that Mr. Matthews, an architect, is an
officer and 50 percent shareholder in Pulliam, Matthews &
Associates, an architectural firm. He serves as the Vice-
President of the city's Cultural Heritage Commission, and as
the Commission's representative on the Interim Design Review
Committee, a group which reviews all construction and exterior
alterations within the Interim Design Review District for
compliance with the city's urban design plan and general
plan.1/

     Your concern is whether a conflict of interest exists,
because Pulliam, Matthews & Associates has been chosen to
jointly develop, with a law firm, a parcel of city-owned
property located within the Interim Design Review District.
Mr. Matthews is a co-partner in the partnership that will
own the building. The floor space will be leased to Pulliam,
Matthews & Associates, the law firm, and other, currently
unknown, limited partners.

     The questions you are concerned about are:

(1)  Does Mr. Matthews have a material financial interest
     in decisions made by the Interim Design Review
     Committee concerning the design of the Pulliam,
     Matthews & Associates Building?


1/   The Committee considers "the height, bulk and siting
of buildings, creation of open spaces, pedestrian amenities
provided, parking facilities, general exterior appearance
and construction, including design, color and texture of
surface materials, and the impact of the proposed project on
nearby cultural heritage buildings."

Victor J. Kaleta
November 9, 1981
Page Two


     (2) Does Mr. Matthews have a material financial interest
         in decisions of the Interim Review Committee concerning
         the construction and alterations of buildings and
         signs within a close proximity to the Pulliam,
         Matthews & Associates Building?

     (3) Does Mr. Matthews have a material financial interest
         in decisions concerning the balance of the Interim
         Design Review District?

     In response to your questions, Government Code Section
87100 2/ provides:

          No public official at any level of state or local
     government shall make, participate in making or in any
     way attempt to use his official position to influence a
     governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
     to know he has a financial interest.

     Mr. Matthews is within the scope of this mandate, for he is
     a member of a local government agency possessing decision-
     making authority. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(1).)

     A definition of the term "financial interest" is provided
     by Section 87103:

          An official has a financial interest in a decision
     within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
     foreseeable that the decision will have a material
     financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
     the public generally, on:

          (a) Any business entity in which the public official
     has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
     thousand dollars ($1,000).

          (b) Any real property in which the public official
     has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
     thousand dollars ($1,000).

          (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
     commercial lending institution in the regular course of
     business on terms available to the public without regard

     _______________
     2/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
     unless otherwise noted.

Victor J. Kaleta
November 9, 1981
Page Three


to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior
to the time when the decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management.

     For purposes of this section, indirect investment
or interest means any investment or interest owned by
. . . a business entity . . . in which the official
. . . own[s] directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-
percent interest or greater.

     Mr. Matthews has the type of financial interests described
in each of the above subsections: he has an investment,
worth more than $1,000, in Pulliam, Matthews & Associates;
the firm is a source of income to him of over $250; and,
Mr. Matthews is a partner in the firm. In addition, once
the building is constructed, Mr. Matthews will have an ownership
interest in this piece of real property.

     In answer to the first question you presented, the
analysis focuses on whether, as a member of the Interim
Design Review Committee, Mr. Matthews will be making decisions,
regarding the Pulliam, Matthews & Associates' building, that
will have a material effect on his financial interests.
"Material financial effect" is explained in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702. (Please refer to enclosed copy.)

     Decisions affecting the cost of constructing the building
could have a material financial effect on the current assets
or liabilities of Pulliam, Matthews & Associates sufficiently
to require Mr. Matthews to disqualify himself. (See 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)(C).) Also, decisions regarding
its construction may materially affect Mr. Matthews' ownership
interest in the building by increasing or decreasing the
fair market value of it by at least $1,000. (See 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(2)(B).) Mr. Matthews is required,
therefore, to disqualify himself from participating in such
decisions. It must be noted, however, that while he will
probably have to abstain from voting on most of these matters,
the Political Reform Act does not require disqualification
where there is no material financial effect, such as a vote
on whether to require the installation of a bicycle rack.

Victor J. Kaleta
November 9, 1981
Page Four


Your second question concerns whether Mr. Matthews will
be making decisions, as to construction in the area immediately
surrounding the Pulliam, Matthews & Associates' building,
which will have a material financial effect on his interests.
Certain decisions could well have such an impact and require
disqualification. An example of this would be a decision to
allow an industrial park to locate in the neighborhood.
This could could have a strong effect on the gross and net
revenues Mr. Matthews receives from Pulliam, Matthews &
Associates because it could generate business for the firm.
Also, a decision, as to whether to allow a skyscraper to be
constructed, could increase or decrease the fair market
value of the Pulliam, Matthews & Associates' building by
$1,000.

On the other hand, there may be many decisions in which
Mr. Matthews could participate. For example, the impact on
the Pulliam, Matthews and Associates' building, of a decision
regarding the color of paint to be used on the surrounding
buildings would appear to be minimal. A case-by-case determination
must be made.

Finally, your last question is whether Mr. Matthews
would have a conflict of interest in making decisions which
affect other areas located within the Interim Design Review
District. The potential for a financial effect resulting
from these decisions is much more attenuated than in previous
questions. Although, arguably, all decisions made in the
District have some effect on the area as a whole, the chance
that these will have a material financial effect on Mr. Matthews'
interests appears slim. There are some decisions, though,
which could have such an effect, an example being the one
cited above, involving the construction of an industrial
park. Such a development could generate greater revenues
for Pulliam, Matthews & Associates, even if not located in
the immediate neighborhood. It is important, though, that
each such decision be scrutinized for a potential conflict
of interest in light of its specific facts.

As this letter has demonstrated, the Political Reform
Act does not require Mr. Matthews to resign from the Interim
Design Review Committee, but rather, it requires him to be
aware of potential conflicts of interest and to disqualify
himself when necessary. Thank you for consulting us on this

Victor J. Kaleta
November 9, 1981
Page Five


issue. If you have any additional questions, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis L. Shank
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JLS:plh
Enclosure

City of Pasadena

ONE HUNDRED NORTH GARFIELD AVENUE
PASADENA, CALIFORNIA 91109

Oct 19 7 50 AM 81

OFFICE OF CITY ATTORNEY
ROOM 202, CITY HALL
PHONE: (213) 577-4141

October 14, 1981


Ms. Barbara Milman, Chief
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Request for Written Advice for
    Mortimer J. Matthews, Member of
    Interim Design Review Committee

Dear Ms. Milman:

This is a request for written advice for Mortimer J. Matthews,
pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Admin.
Code Section 18329.

Factual Background

Mr. Matthews, an architect, is the Vice-Chairman of the City's
Cultural Heritage Commission and serves as its representative
on the Interim Design Review Committee established by
Ordinance No. 5491, a copy of which is attached hereto. The
Interim Design Review Committee reviews all new construction
and exterior alterations within the Interim Design Review
District within the Central Business District. The Interim
Design Review District is defined as that area from Pasadena
Avenue on the east to Wilson Avenue, 210 Freeway to California
exclusive of Old Pasadena. Ordinance No. 5491 contains a map of the
District. Additionally, I have enclosed a marked aerial
photograph of the District, a marked map with potential
development, and a marked street map.

Mr. Matthews is an officer and a 50 percent shareholder in
Pulliam, Matthews & Associates which has been selected to
develop a parcel of City property at the northwest corner
of Walnut Street and Marengo Avenue, a property within the
Interim Design Review District. The location is shown on the

Page 2 - Barbara Milman                October 14, 1981


marked street map.

This office has opined that Mr. Matthews is disqualified
from reviewing the design of the Pulliam, Matthews &
Associates building and any construction within close
proximity to said building as shown on the marked street
map. As to the balance of the Interim Design Review
District, this office could not foresee a financial
interest but recommended that Mr. Matthews resign from
the Interim Design Review Committee to avoid potential
conflict of interest charges.

Questions Posed

The specific questions Mr. Matthews requires written advice
on are as follows:

     1.  Does Mr. Matthews have a financial interest in
         decisions of the Interim Design Review
         Committee as concerns the design of the Pullian,
         Matthews & Associates building?

     2.  Does Mr. Matthews have a financial interest in
         such decisions as concerns construction and
         alterations within close proximity to said
         building as shown on the marked street map?

     3.  Does Mr. Matthews have a financial interest
         in such decisions as concerns the balance of
         the Interim Design Review District?

If you require further factual material to answer the above
questions, please contact the undersigned.

Very truly yours,

Evelynn M. Finn
City Attorney


Victor J. Kaleta
Deputy City Attorney

VJK:jw

cc: Mortimer Matthews
    Jane Ellison

Encls.

sections.conclusion: The political Reform Act does not require Mr. Matthews to resign from the Interim Design Review Committee, but rather, it requires him to be aware of potential conflicts of interest and to disqualify himself when necessary. Mr. Matthews is required to disqualify himself from participating in decisions affecting the cost and fair market value of the Pulliam, Matthews & Associates building. sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mortimer Matthews, an officer and 50% shareholder in an architectural firm selected to develop city property within an Interim Design Review District, has financial conflicts of interest that would require him to disqualify himself from committee decisions regarding his firm's building, nearby construction, and the broader district.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Matthews must disqualify himself from decisions directly affecting his building's construction costs and fair market value, must evaluate case-by-case decisions regarding proximate construction, and generally may participate in decisions affecting the broader district unless they would materially affect his financial interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (pages with Victor Kaleta's incoming questions) and FPPC response letter (James L. Shank's advice). OCR quality issues present but content substantially legible. Question and conclusion sections clearly identifiable. Date in signature shows November 9, 1991 but header indicates 1981; 1981 is correct based on document ID 81A506 and letter content dates.

QUESTION: s.
Although, arguably, all decisions made in the
District have some effect on the area as
a whole, the chance
that these will have a material financial effect on Mr. Matthews-
interests appears slim.
There are some decisions, though,
which could have such an effect, an example being the one
cited above, involving the construction of an industrial
park,
Such a development could generate greater revenues
for Pulliam, Matthews
a Associates, even if not located in
the immediate neighborhood.
it is important, though, that
each such decis;on be scrutinized for a potential conflict
of interest in light of its specific facts.
ns this letter has demonstrated,
the political Reform
Act does not require Mr. Matthews to resign from the lnterim
Design Review committee, but rather,
it reguircs him to be
aware of potential conflicts of interest and to disqualify
himself when necessary.
Thank you for consulting us on this


i
‘
Victor J. Kaleza
November 9, 1991
Page Five
issue.
If you have any add1t1onal qucstlons, plaase feel
free to Contact me at (915) 322—5901.

CONCLUSION: The political Reform Act does not require Mr. Matthews to resign from the Interim Design Review Committee, but rather, it requires him to be aware of potential conflicts of interest and to disqualify himself when necessary. Mr. Matthews is required to disqualify himself from participating in decisions affecting the cost and fair market value of the Pulliam, Matthews & Associates building. For decisions regarding construction in the immediate vicinity, case-by-case determination is necessary based on material financial effects. For decisions affecting other areas of the District, the potential for material financial effect is attenuated, though some decisions (like industrial park development) could require disqualification.

Kaleta Deputy City Attorney 100 North Garfield Avenue Pasadena, CA 91109 Dear Mr. Kaleta: I am writing in response to your question concerning a member of Pasadena's Interim Design Review Commission. The specific facts are that Mr. Matthews, an architect, is an officer and 50 percent shareholder in Pulliam, Matthews & Associates, an architectural firm. He serves as the Vice- President of the city's Cultural Heritage Commission, and as the Commission's representative on the Interim Design Review Committee, a group which reviews all construction and exterior alterations within the Interim Design Review District for compliance with the city's urban design plan and general plan.1/ Your concern is whether a conflict of interest exists, because Pulliam, Matthews & Associates has been chosen to jointly develop, with a law firm, a parcel of city-owned property located within the Interim Design Review District. Mr. Matthews is a co-partner in the partnership that will own the building. The floor space will be leased to Pulliam, Matthews & Associates, the law firm, and other, currently unknown, limited partners. The questions you are concerned about are: (1) Does Mr. Matthews have a material financial interest in decisions made by the Interim Design Review Committee concerning the design of the Pulliam, Matthews & Associates Building? 1/ The Committee considers "the height, bulk and siting of buildings, creation of open spaces, pedestrian amenities provided, parking facilities, general exterior appearance and construction, including design, color and texture of surface materials, and the impact of the proposed project on nearby cultural heritage buildings." Victor J. Kaleta November 9, 1981 Page Two (2) Does Mr. Matthews have a material financial interest in decisions of the Interim Review Committee concerning the construction and alterations of buildings and signs within a close proximity to the Pulliam, Matthews & Associates Building? (3) Does Mr. Matthews have a material financial interest in decisions concerning the balance of the Interim Design Review District? In response to your questions, Government Code Section 87100 2/ provides: No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Mr. Matthews is within the scope of this mandate, for he is a member of a local government agency possessing decision- making authority. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(1).) A definition of the term "financial interest" is provided by Section 87103: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Mortimer Matthews, an architect and officer/50% shareholder in Pulliam, Matthews & Associates, has financial interests requiring disqualification under Government Code Section 87100 from certain decisions of the Interim Design Review Committee, but does not need to resign, only to disqualify himself on a case-by-case basis where material financial effects exist.
